data.title: Q Logistics Offer for Aramex Exceeds Minimum Threshold
data.description:
Q Logistics said Friday the acceptance of its takeover offer for Aramex's (DFM:ARMX) shares not held by Abu Dhabi Ports (ADX:ADPORTS), d/b/a AD Ports Group, exceeded the minimum acceptance threshold.

The Abu Dhabi Developmental Holding, or ADQ, unit received acceptances for 40.57% of Aramex's shares after extending the acceptance period of its takeover offer. The offer, combined with AD Ports Group's existing 22.69% stake, resulted in an aggregate holding of 63.26%.

However, the finalization of the deal depends on fulfilling the offer document conditions.

data.title: CSPC Pharmaceutical's 2024 Profit Declines 25%; Shares Jump 3%
data.description:
CSPC Pharmaceutical's attributable profit fell 25% to 4.68 billion yuan in 2024 from 6.28 billion yuan in 2023, according to a Friday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

Earnings per share at the drug company decreased 26% to 0.3687 yuan from 0.4947 yuan a year earlier. Analysts at Visible Alpha estimated EPS at 0.40 yuan.

Revenue declined 7.8% to 29 billion yuan from 31.5 billion yuan in the previous year. Visible Alpha analysts forecast revenue at 29.4 billion yuan.

Directors recommended a final dividend of HK$0.10 per share, payable July 18 to shareholders on record as of June 9.

Shares rose 3% during Friday's afternoon trading.

data.title: Capitec Bank CEO Gerrie Fourie to retire in July
data.description: South Africa's Capitec Bank said on Friday that Group CEO and founding member Gerrie Fourie will retire in July, and named Graham Lee, the group executive of its retail bank division, as his successor.

data.title: EuroSports Global Repurchases Shares
data.description:
EuroSports Global repurchased 30,000 shares on the market for SG$4,530 or SG$0.15 apiece on Monday, a same-day filing with the Singapore Exchange said.

The company has so far repurchased 1.9 million shares out of the 24,552,810 shares it is authorized to repurchase under its current mandate.

data.title: Here’s what happened in crypto today
data.description:
Today in crypto, the Securities and Exchange Commission nominee Paul Atkins faced a Senate confirmation hearing, while a new survey showed that fewer than 1 in 5 European banks currently offer crypto services — despite rising investor demand. Meanwhile, the US Senate voted to repeal the DeFi broker rule.

Prospective SEC chair pressed on sale of FTX-tied firm

Lawmakers in the US Senate Banking Committee questioned prospective Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) member Paul Atkins on his ties to the crypto industry and how he might regulate digital assets if confirmed.

Questioning Atkins at his nomination hearing on March 27, Massachusetts Senator Elizabeth Warren, the committee’s ranking member, said the former SEC commissioner had had “staggeringly bad judgment” in his role leading up to the 2008 financial crisis — Atkins served at the agency from 2002 to 2008. 

Sen. Warren also asked Atkins to disclose the buyers of his consulting firm Patomak Global Partners — which advised crypto exchange FTX before its collapse in 2022 — for transparency about potential conflicts of interest with the digital asset industry.

“Your clients pay you north of $1,200 an hour for advice on how to influence regulators like the SEC, and if you’re confirmed, you will be in a prime spot to deliver for all those clients who’ve been paying you millions of dollars for years,” said Sen. Warren, suggesting Atkins’ judgment “will be influenced by more than an objective assessment of the data.”

The Massachusetts senator sent a letter to Donald Trump’s SEC pick on March 23, calling on him to be prepared to answer questions related to his potential role at the agency based on his ties to the crypto industry through Patomak. At the March 27 hearing, Sen. Warren asked Atkins to disclose the consulting firm’s potential buyers — he said he planned to sell the company if confirmed — who might be “buying access to the future chair of the SEC.” 

Atkins said he would “abide by the process” but did not directly answer Sen. Warren’s question. She suggested that the sale of Patomak could be a “pre-bribe” for the former SEC commissioner’s services.

Most EU banks fail to meet rising crypto investor demand — Survey

European banks and financial institutions may be underestimating the demand for cryptocurrency services, with fewer than one in five offering digital asset products, according to a new survey by crypto investment platform Bitpanda.

The study surveyed 10,000 retail and business investors across 13 European countries and found that more than 40% of business investors already hold cryptocurrencies, with another 18% planning to invest in the near future.

Yet, only 19% of surveyed financial institutions said their clients showed strong demand for crypto products, suggesting a 30% gap between actual investor adoption and perceived interest.

Moreover, only 19% of surveyed European financial institutions are offering crypto services, while over 80% of institutions acknowledge crypto’s growing importance.

Still, some European banks are recognizing the growing demand for digital assets, with 18% of surveyed financial institutions planning to expand their crypto service offering, particularly offerings related to crypto transfers.

Senate sends resolution killing IRS DeFi broker rule to Trump

The US Senate passed a resolution on March 26 to revoke a Biden-era rule that would have required decentralized finance (DeFi) protocols to report to the Internal Revenue Service, which will now head to President Donald Trump, who has supported killing the rule.

The Senate voted 70-28 to pass the motion to repeal the so-called IRS DeFi broker rule that aimed to require DeFi platforms, such as decentralized exchanges, to file their gross proceeds from crypto sales and include information on those involved in the transactions.

The White House’s AI and crypto czar, David Sacks, has previously said Trump supports killing the rule.

Critics of the rule said it would lump decentralized platforms with too onerous rules, which would hamper crypto innovation, while those who opposed the resolution said it would create a tax evasion loophole.

The Senate was widely expected to pass the resolution as it originally passed a version of it in early March. The House made a copycat due to Constitutional budget rules, which it passed on March 11.

data.title: Apple, Meta to Get Minimal Fines Under New EU Digital Antitrust Rules, FT Says, Citing Sources
data.description:
• iPhone maker Apple and Facebook owner Meta are set to get minimal fines from the European Commission next week under its digital antitrust rules, the FT reports, citing unnamed sources.
  • Alongside the fines the FT says that Apple will be ordered to revise its App Store rules while Meta will be ordered to change its pay or consent model.
  • Under the Digital Markets Act, companies the EU designates as "gatekeepers" aren't allowed to treat their own services more favorably in ranking than similar services of third parties.
  • The commission declined to comment to the FT.

Full story: https://tinyurl.com/bdd7m8d8

Write to Ian Walker at ian.walker@wsj.com

data.title: China's H3C warns of Nvidia AI chip shortage amid surging demand
data.description:
Key points:
  • Server maker H3C warns of depleting inventory of H20 chips
  • Demand for Nvidia's H20 chips surges as AI efforts boost orders
  • New shipments expected mid-April
  • H3C to distribute limited supply on basis of profit margins

One of China’s largest server makers, H3C, has flagged potential shortages of Nvidia's H20 chip, the most advanced AI processor legally available domestically under U.S. export controls, in a client notice seen by Reuters.

The potential supply crunch could create obstacles for China's artificial intelligence ambitions at a time when its tech firms are aggressively expanding their investments in AI.

"H20's international supply chain faces significant uncertainties," the company said in Tuesday's notice, adding that current inventory was nearly depleted.

Geopolitical tension that is roiling global trade and supply of key materials was responsible for the uncertainty, it added, saying new shipments were expected by mid-April this year.

H3C did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment. After publication, an Nvidia spokesperson said the company declined to comment.

Demand for H20 chips has surged in recent months as companies rush to adopt Chinese AI startup DeepSeek's cost-effective AI models.

Tencent , Alibaba and ByteDance have significantly increased orders of the H20 since the Chinese AI startup burst into the global public consciousness since January, Reuters reported last month.

The Chinese server maker said supply plans beyond April 20 also face uncertainties from raw material policy changes, shipping disruptions, and production challenges.

In the notice, H3C said it would distribute incoming H20 chips on the basis of a profit-first principle, prioritising stable, long-term customers with higher profit margins on their orders.

H20 processors are currently scarce in the Chinese market, said an industry source, who distributes AI servers including those that make use of H20 chips.

"We were told the chips would be available, but when it came time to actually purchase them, we were informed they had already been sold at higher prices," added the source, who sought anonymity given the information is private.

U.S. officials were considering curbs on sales of H20 chips to China, Reuters reported in January.

H3C is a major OEM partner for Nvidia's AI chips in China, alongside others such as Inspur, Lenovo and Huawei's spin-off x86 server unit, xFusion.

The primary chip Nvidia is legally permitted to sell in China, the H20 was launched after the latest round of U.S. export restrictions took effect in October 2023.

Washington has banned exports of Nvidia's most advanced chips to China since 2022, concerned that its rival could use advanced technologies to build up its military capabilities.

Analysts estimate Nvidia shipped about 1 million H20 units in 2024, generating revenue of more than $12 billion for the company.

Huawei and Cambricon offer domestic alternatives to the H20.

data.title: Qifu Technology Completes $690 Million Convertible Notes Offering
data.description:
Qifu Technology completed a $690 million convertible senior notes offering due 2030, including a $90 million additional purchase option, according to a Friday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The notes bear an annual interest rate of 0.50%, payable semiannually, and have an initial conversion price of $59.71 per ADS.

Proceeds will support a newly established share repurchase plan and the November 2024 buyback.

The credit technology services provider's shares were up 2% in recent trading.

data.title: Soltech's Spanish subsidiary installs and commissions the Balearic Islands' largest floating solar installation
data.description:
Soltech's Spanish subsidiary Sud Renovables is now commissioning Soltech's and the Balearic Islands' largest floating solar installation to date. The solar panels are installed on top of a rainwater collection pond used for irrigation. The installed capacity is about 1.5 MWp and the solar panels cover an area of just over 9,000 sqm.

The Spanish solar energy company Sud Renovables, which became part of the Soltech Group in 2022, is active in solar energy and storage solutions for self-consumption of solar energy. The projects often consist of large-scale solar facades, roof-mounted solar systems, solar roofs for parking spaces and floating installations.

The company has now designed and installed the Balearic Islands' largest floating solar installation on a rainwater collection pond in Capdepera, Mallorca. The investment is part of the municipality's investments in renewable energy. The installation will also include bird protection devices to prevent interference from birds.

– After constructing the first floating installation in Catalonia, we are very proud to continue to grow in this type of solar installation, now in Mallorca. Floating solutions are very important in our area in southern Europe to help save water and as an alternative to using agricultural land. This is further proof that solar technology is fully compatible with agricultural operations, says Manel Romero, Chief Technology Officer and Co-Founder at Sud Renovables.

Reduces evaporation and increases the power of solar panels

Previously, Sud Renovables designed and installed Catalonia's first floating solar system on top of a rainwater collection pond. This type of solution is expected to grow in number in the future as floating solar energy installations have a number of advantages, in addition to producing renewable solar electricity.

– First of all, I would like to congratulate Sud Renovables for completing a very inspiring project. Floating solar systems reduce evaporation of water dams, which is important in regions prone to drought. The water, in turn, helps to cool down the solar panels, which increases their output by 10-15 percent. We expect an increased demand for this type of installation and it feels great that our subsidiary is an industry leader in the field, says Patrik Hahne, CEO of Soltech Energy.Photo: Sud Renovables/G-ener Renovables

data.title: SocGen appoints Alexis Kohler as executive vice president
data.description:
French banking giant Societe Generale announced on Friday the appointment of Alexis Kohler as executive vice president, effective June 2025.

Kohler, whose previous roles include General Secretary of the Presidency of the French Republic, will be responsible for leading M&A, Equity Capital Markets and Acquisition Finance activities, as well as assisting CEO Slawomir Krupa in implementing transformation programmes within the bank.

data.title: Wihlborgs Signs Agreement With IWG
data.description:

  • WIHLBORGS SIGNS AGREEMENT WITH IWG FOR FLEXIBLE WORKPLACES AND SERVICES IN "VISTA," HYLLIE

data.title: Nikkei Declines as Japan Braces for Trump's Tariff Plan
data.description:
Japanese shares fell Friday as investors cut risk ahead of Trump's plan to unveil reciprocal tariffs on April 2, after slapping a 25% levy on auto imports.

The Nikkei 225 dropped 1.95%, or 738.77 points, to 37,061.20.

Trump's tariffs, aimed at boosting US manufacturing, are expected to shrink Japan's economy by about 0.2%, according to Nomura's Takahide Kiuchi.

The contraction could hit domestic production and auto sector jobs.

The Japanese government has formally requested an exemption, pointing to Japanese automakers' contributions to the US economy through investments and job creation. Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba said Japan is considering all options, including countermeasures.

Adding to the pressure, Tokyo's inflation quickened to 2.9% in March from 2.8% in February, data from Japan's Statistics Bureau showed.

Core CPI, which excludes fresh food prices, rose 2.4% from 2.2%, as higher food prices offset lower utility costs from subsidies.

On the corporate front, Companion Holdings delayed AP78 Co.'s tender offer for Furukawa Battery to late June due to Japan's antitrust review.

Companion plans to buy back 18.78 million shares and sees no change to its outlook.

I-mobile will transfer its electricity retail business to Furunavi Energy on April 1, with the split taking effect on June 1. Furunavi Energy will manage related assets and liabilities.

Resonac Holdings posted a 73.5 billion yen profit in 2024, rebounding from a 6.51 billion yen loss. EPS climbed to 406.61 yen from a 35.91 yen loss. Revenue grew 7.4% to 1.391 trillion yen.

data.title: French and Benelux stocks-Factors to watch
data.description:
Below are company-related news and stories from France and Benelux which could have an impact on the region's markets or individual stocks.

EXMAR :

Exmar on Thursday reported a decrease in FY IFRS revenue to $348.9 million but a rise in IFRS EBITDA to $204.7 million. It will not propose a dividend for 2024.

FLUXYS :

Fluxys reported on Thursday increasing FY revenues and EBIT to 608.8 million euros ($657.0 million) and 133.9 million euros respectively. It proposes a dividend of EUR 1.4 per share.

HAL TRUST :

HAL Trust's NAV increased by 2.20 billion euros to 15.50 billion euros at the end of 2024, with the holding's FY net income at 1.21 billion euros, it reported on Thursday.

SANOFI

French drugmaker said on Friday its antibody Dupixent had been approved as the first-ever biologic medicine in Japan for patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).

SOCIETE GENERALE

French bank said on Friday it had appointed of Alexis Kohler , current general secretary of the French presidency, as Executive Vice President, adding he would join the compay in June 2025 and that he will be a member of the group executive committee.

UBISOFT :

French videogame maker Ubisoft said on Thursday it has set up a subsidiary in which China's Tencent will invest 1.16 billion euros ($1.25 billion).

data.title: Merck KGaA Exercises Global Commercialization Option for Pimicotinib
data.description:
Merck KGaA exercised an option with China's Abbisko Therapeutics Co. to commercialize pimicotinib in the US and the rest of the world.

The German science and technology company will pay an option fee of $85 million to Abbisko under an agreement signed in 2023, according to a Friday release. Pimicotinib is intended to treat patients with tenosynovial giant cell tumor.

Merck added that the companies also plan to explore the use of pimicotinib for other indications, including chronic graft-versus-host disease.

data.title: India criticises X for calling compliance website a censorship tool
data.description:
India has criticised Elon Musk's X for wrongly labelling as a "censorship portal" an official website that allows tech companies to be notified about harmful online content, court papers reviewed by Reuters showed on Friday.

The lawsuit marks an escalation in a legal dispute between X and the government of Prime Minister Narendra Modi over how it orders content to be taken down, at a time when Musk nears the launch in India of his other key ventures, Starlink and Tesla.

The comments came in a rejoinder to X's new lawsuit filed on March 5 in the southern state of Karnataka seeking to quash the government's website initiative.

"The use of the said terminology by a worldwide portal like X is unfortunate and condemnable," the information technology ministry told the court, adding that the company was raising a "groundless concern of censorship".

The ministry, whose statement to the court on March 27 is not public, did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

Modi's government says the new website was intended only to swiftly notify tech companies of their due diligence obligations and no blocking orders were issued.

X says India has unlawfully expanded its censorship powers to allow easier removal of online content, giving "countless" government officials the power to execute such orders through the website it described as a "censorship portal".

data.title: Strong earthquake in central Myanmar, panic in Bangkok
data.description:
An earthquake of magnitude 6.9 struck Myanmar, the German Research Centre for Geosciences (GFZ) said on Friday, while hundreds of people poured out of buildings in the Thai capital Bangkok after the tremors.

There was no immediate word from Myanmar on damage after the quake, which GFZ said was at a depth of 10 km (6.21 miles) with the epicentre near the city of Mandalay.

Witnesses in Bangkok said people ran out onto the streets in panic and water splashed out of swimming pools.

data.title: European Markets Poised for Lower Open
data.description:
European equity markets were set for a slightly negative open on Friday as investors braced for a raft of economic reports from the region.

Key data releases include German consumer confidence, British retail sales, and French inflation, alongside Eurozone economic sentiment and consumer inflation expectations.

On the corporate front, earnings results are expected from UK companies NEXT and Adriatic Metals.

Meanwhile, investor caution persisted ahead of US President Donald Trump’s auto tariffs, set to take effect next week, which are expected to heavily impact European carmakers.

In premarket trade, Euro Stoxx 50 and Stoxx 600 futures were both down about 0.1%.

data.title: Chariot Unit Extends Payment Terms in Black Mtn. Lithium Deal, Adds Share-Based Option
data.description:
Chariot said its unit, Panther Lithium Corporation, entered into a second amendment to the amended and restated exploration and secured option agreement with Black Mtn. Lithium, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

Under the revised second amendment terms, the original obligation for Panther to pay $1.45 million in three cash installments by Dec. 30 has been extended to seven cash installments due by Sept. 30, 2026, and the issue of 2 million fully paid ordinary shares of Chariot, the filing added.

If shareholders do not approve of the deal, the company will pay Black Mtn. Lithium cash equal the value of 2 million Chariot shares, based on the 10-day volume-weighted average price on the shareholder meeting date, within 30 days.

data.title: South Korea's Kospi Falls 1.9% Amid Persisting U.S. Tariff Concerns — Market Talk
data.description:
0645 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi fell 1.9% to close at 2557.98 amid persisting U.S. tariff concerns. The index lost 3.2% for the week, snapping a three-week gaining streak. President Trump is due to unveil reciprocal duties on April 2 after announcing plans earlier this week to impose 25% auto tariffs. Foreign and institutional investors were net sellers in Friday's session. Semiconductor and auto stocks retreated. Index heavyweight Samsung Electronics fell 2.6%. Memory-chip maker SK Hynix lost 3.7%. Carmaker Hyundai Motor slid 3.5%. USD/KRW settled 1.1% higher at 1,466.50 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield was flat at 2.838%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0629 GMT - A majority of European Central Bank Governing Council members are expected to vote in favor of a 25-basis-point interest-rate cut in April, says SEB Research strategist Jussi Hiljanen in a note. SEB Research expects a 25-basis-point rate cut, barring an upside surprise in April inflation, he says. A rate cut next month is likely even as various GC members recently suggested that at least some of the hawkish members will likely oppose a rate cut in April, he says. Money markets price in a 81% probability of a 25-basis-point cut in April, according to LSEG. A 25-basis-point rate cut would lower the ECB's deposit facility rate to 2.25%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0622 GMT - Interest-rate cuts by the European Central Bank currently help to ease the upside pressure in long-dated government bond yields coming from more expansionary German fiscal policy, SEB Research's Jussi Hiljanen says in a note. "However, this may change rapidly once markets become convinced that the ECB has finalised the rate cutting cycle," he says. At that point, the market focus will shift to the timing of the first rate hike and expectations will begin facilitating higher long rates rather than limiting their increase, he says. For investors, this means that long eurozone government bond yields don't have any room for structural downside and any dips should be considered as opportunities to position for higher long rates, Hiljanen says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0517 GMT - Activity in China's vast manufacturing sector likely expanded in March despite tariff hits from the U.S., in part thanks to Beijing's stimulus push. The official manufacturing PMI is expected to come in at 50.4 for March, up from February's 50.2, according to a poll of economists by The Wall Street Journal. That would mark the second straight month that the gauge has risen above the 50 mark that separates activity expansion from contraction. China's National Bureau of Statistics is scheduled to release the data Monday. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

0428 GMT - Nomura economists suspect the criteria for U.S. reciprocal tariffs will be much broader than simply matching other countries' tariffs currently imposed on the U.S. Under the most narrow interpretation of matching tariffs, the analysts say India, Thailand and Brazil are among the most exposed. "However, we suspect the criteria for U.S. reciprocal tariffs will be much broader than that, and indeed more difficult to quantify," they say. Investigations by U.S. departments are due to be completed by April 1 and cover many other areas: trade deficits with other countries, de minimis exports, VAT rates, digital taxes, currency manipulation, state subsidies and various other non-tariff trade barriers. There is massive uncertainty surrounding the targets, size and scope of the tariffs expected to be unveiled on April 2, they add. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0410 GMT - The Reserve Bank of Australia's statement at its coming April 1 meeting is unlikely to be a market mover for the Aussie dollar, TD Securities says, as it expects no change in tone. The RBA is unlikely to shift its 'hawkish' stance and isn't likely to signal an intention to cut as early as May, strategists Prashant Newnaha and Alex Loo say in a note. They see April 2 as the more market-moving event. Trump's reciprocal tariff plans are expected to weigh heavily on the Asian region as 25% of the U.S. trade deficit is with Asia. Australia's strong trade ties to Asia leave it vulnerable, they say, and anticipate the AUD to trade on the back foot versus the USD, revisiting the range lows at 0.62 by end-2Q. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0408 GMT - Indian shares are lower as concerns surrounding U.S. tariffs persist. Tech stocks lead the declines, with Infosys down 1.6% and HCL Technologies lower by 0.8%. Among individual movers, Force Motors is up 4.5% after winning a contract to supply nearly 3,000 vehicles to Indian defence forces. Investors are focusing on potential developments related to U.S. trade and foreign policies and their implications for Indian businesses. The Sensex edges down 0.1% at 77500.24. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0401 GMT - Taiwan could face smaller tariff hikes due to its investments in the U.S. and key tech exports, says Lynn Song, ING's Greater China chief economist, in a note. Taiwan has been under scrutiny due to its rising trade surplus with the U.S. over the past years, which amounted to $64.9 billion in 2024. However, Taiwan's key exports of semiconductors and computer products to the U.S. have limited replacement alternatives, raising the probability of being exempted, the economist says. Also, TSMC's expanding investment in the U.S. "may have also brought Taiwan enough goodwill to soften or avert tariffs," he says. As a result, Taiwan could be one of the more likely economies to benefit from a tariff reprieve, or at least smaller tariff hikes, he adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0335 GMT - Ahead of "Liberation Day" in the U.S. as dubbed by President Trump, Australians are looking out to the horizon with trepidation, knowing that a trade war would badly expose the fact that the country relies on the U.S. for its security, while looking to Beijing for its prosperity. Trump is set to announce so-called reciprocal tariffs on Wednesday. With tariffs on cars already announced, other sectoral tariffs will likely be announced in time, says Khoon Goh, head of Asia research at ANZ. While there is scope for negotiations over tariffs, trade uncertainty will remain high and Asia's exports will likely slow, he says. China's attempt to boost consumption will help offset weakness in its exports but may not be sufficient to support the wider region, Goh adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0331 GMT - The Malaysian ringgit is likely to remain range-bound next week, trading between 4.42 and 4.45 against the U.S. dollar, despite ongoing uncertainties surrounding Trump's tariff strategy, Kenanga IB economists say in a note. While the risk-off sentiment may weigh on the ringgit, Malaysia appears relatively insulated and better positioned to withstand U.S. pressures compared with its regional peers, they reckon. Technically, the pair's outlook appears neutral and could hover around 4.432, they add. Kenanga pegs USD/MYR's short-term resistance at 4.436 and support at 4.428. USD/MYR is flat at 4.4320.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0308 GMT - South Korea's export growth was likely sustained in March, after a rebound in February. Exports are tipped to have grown 1.7% from a year earlier, following a revised 0.7% gain in February, according to the median forecast of seven economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. Strong-than-expected shipments of semiconductors and ships may have helped maintain growth, but no solid rebound is likely given the weak recovery in exports to China as well as fresh U.S. tariffs that are expected to take effect in April, iM Securities chief economist Park Sang-hyun says. Imports likely gained 0.7% on year, to result in a $6.00 billion trade surplus in March, following a revised $4.15 billion surplus in the prior month, the poll shows. Trade data are due Tuesday. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0246 GMT - Australia's federal election on May 3 will be a watershed moment for the future of the country's energy supply. Anxiety over soaring electricity costs and business failures has been a major issue in Canberra for some time. The election will determine whether the country's future lies with renewable energy sources under Labor or with a diverse mix of power sources, including nuclear, under the coalition. The coalition also plans to redirect gas typically earmarked for export back into the domestic market. It's a hot-button issue for companies that require energy certainty and something that must be resolved quickly for a country that is otherwise a major supplier of cheap, reliable energy to other parts of the world. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.title: Pre-announcement of a financial report according to articles 114 - 117 WpHG
data.description:
OVB Holding AG

by Brigitte Bonifer

Pre-announcement of a financial report according to articles 114 - 117 WpHG

OVB Holding AG: Pre-announcement of a financial report according to articles 114 - 117 WpHG

Cologne (pta000/28.03.2025/07:45 UTC+1) - OVB Holding AG announces that the following financial reports shall be disclosed:

data.title: China International Marine Containers Cancels Spin-Off, Listing of CIMC-TianDa on ChiNext
data.description:
China International Marine Containers (Group) terminated the proposed spin-off and listing of its controlling subsidiary, CIMC-TianDa Holdings, on the ChiNext Board of the Shenzhen Stock Exchange.

The decision, approved on March 27, follows comprehensive evaluation and coordination of business development and capital operations. The spin-off and listing were initially approved at the third extraordinary general meeting on June 18, 2021.

CIMC believes the termination will not impact its financial condition or strategic plans. The listing application documents have been withdrawn.
